nativeRange.habitat: The Squaretail Bristletooth Tang inhabits sheltered inner reef crests and slopes at depths of roughly 1–21 m, typically encountered singly or in small groups grazing detritus and fine algal films from hard substrate.

decorPreferences: live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
open swimming space along the length of the tank
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
long footprint (4–5 ft minimum) preferred over tall depth

diet.notes: Uses individually movable bristle-like teeth to sift detritus and graze algal films from live rock. Marine-based seaweed (nori) and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week; vitamin-rich diets are commonly recommended to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion. Has been documented accepting algae-based flakes/pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and occasional meaty foods (mysis shrimp) in mixed-community feedings.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 5076x3384. Tack-sharp full-body side profile of an adult in Zanzibar; clearly shows the diagnostic bright gold eye-ring, fine pale body spotting, yellow fin accents and the namesake squared-off (truncate) caudal fin.
Francois Libert, 1000x665. Striking sharp adult with dark purple-brown body, electric blue-white spotting, glowing gold eye-ring and gold pectoral fin; excellent display of diagnostic coloration.
Diego Delso, 4766x4766. Sharp full-body reddish-brown individual showing colour variation, gold eye-ring, pale spotting and squared caudal fin; adds variety to the gallery.

summary: The Squaretail Bristletooth Tang is a small Indian Ocean surgeonfish valued in reef aquariums for its specialised bristle-like teeth, which make it an efficient grazer of detritus and algal films. It is generally considered reef-safe and tends to be peaceful toward non-tang tankmates.
careNotes:
The Squaretail Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus truncatus) is one of the smaller members of the Ctenochaetus genus, typically reaching around 15–17 cm in captivity. Its species name, truncatus, refers to the distinctively squared-off caudal fin — an unusual trait that sets it apart from most other bristletooth tangs, which taper to a point.

A tank of at least 300 L with a long horizontal footprint (roughly 4–5 ft) is generally advised. Bristletooth tangs are active swimmers that graze throughout the day, and a generous tank length gives them natural grazing routes along the rockwork. Plenty of live rock colonised with mature biofilm and algal turf is considered important, as the species' fine, bristle-like teeth are specialised for sifting these organic films from hard surfaces — a behaviour that also makes them effective at keeping detritus from accumulating on rock.

Water parameters should be kept stable within standard reef ranges: specific gravity around 1.022–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, and dKH 8–12.

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae. Dried seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based herbivore preparations are generally recommended at least three times per week. Vitamin-enriched foods are commonly suggested to reduce the risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — a condition in which the sensory pores along the head and lateral line develop pitting and discolouration. Most acanthurids (the surgeonfish family) are susceptible to HLLE when kept on a poor diet or in chronically poor water quality, and the Squaretail Bristletooth Tang is no exception. It has been documented accepting algae-based flakes and pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and occasional meaty foods such as mysis shrimp during mixed community feedings.

The species is generally considered reef-safe. Isolated reports note occasional grazing on some soft corals and sessile invertebrates, but well-fed individuals tend not to pick at corals. It is best kept one per tank: conspecific aggression toward other Ctenochaetus bristletooth tangs and similarly shaped surgeonfish tends to be high, while it is generally peaceable with unrelated reef fish.

Like all surgeonfish, this species carries sharp retractable blades at the base of the tail (the caudal peduncle) — the feature that gives the family the common name 'surgeonfish'. These blades are not venomous but can inflict painful lacerations on handlers and tankmates during stress or conflict; caution is warranted during net transfers.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. Like most surgeonfish, the Squaretail Bristletooth Tang is presumed to reproduce in the wild through pelagic broadcast spawning — releasing eggs and sperm into open water where the larvae develop in the plankton. Replicating the conditions needed to trigger spawning, let alone raising the larvae through their planktonic phase, is considered beyond practical reach in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Squaretail Bristletooth Tang. Provided max size (6.5 in / 16.5 cm), tank minimum (70 gal / 265 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore, seaweed 3x/week), temperament (semi-aggressive toward other tangs, peaceful otherwise), reef compatibility (yes), and family (Acanthuridae).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity, family (Acanthuridae) and order (Acanthuriformes), max size (16 cm SL), depth (1–21 m), native range (Indian Ocean — sheltered inner reef crests and slopes), preferred temperature range (24.9–29.3°C, mean ~28.2°C), IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010), and common names (Indian gold-ring bristle-tooth, Yelloweye bristletooth).

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Designated Reef2Reef wiki secondary returned HTTP 403; substituted this hobby guide. Confirmed adult size (~6 in / 15 cm), minimum tank (90 gal / 340 L, larger than LiveAquaria's 70 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), semi-aggressive temperament toward other Ctenochaetus, reef-safe, and Indian Ocean (East Africa to Sri Lanka) range.

additional.name: ReefBay Square Tail Bristletooth Tang Care Guide
additional.notes: Additional hobby guide. Suggested 10 in max (likely overstated — outlier above FishBase 16 cm SL), 125+ gal minimum (used as the upper bound for the cautious-larger tank rule), semi-aggressive with territorial behaviour toward similar species, reef compatible with caveats (may graze some soft corals).

additional.name: Freakin' Corals
additional.notes: Reef hobby retailer page. Confirmed 6.5 in max size, 70 gal minimum tank, standard reef parameters, high conspecific aggression (one per tank, may be harassed by its own species), Africa/Sri Lanka range, herbivorous diet with seaweed 3x/week.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found in available hobby sources. Like most acanthurids, presumed pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild; spawning in aquaria is considered highly unlikely.
